## **Learning with Noisy Correspondence for Cross-modal Matching**

**Zhenyu Huang** _[∗]_ College of Computer Science Sichuan University, China `zyhuang.gm@gmail.com`

**Guocheng Niu** Baidu Inc., China `niuguocheng@baidu.com`

## **Xiao Liu**

TAL Education Group `liuxiao15@tal.com`

**Wenbiao Ding** TAL Education Group `dingwenbiao@tal.com`

**Xinyan Xiao** Baidu Inc., China `xiaoxinyan@baidu.com`

## **Hua Wu**

Baidu Inc., China `wu_hua@baidu.com`

**Xi Peng** _[†]_ College of Computer Science Sichuan University, China `pengx.gm@gmail.com`

## **Abstract**

Cross-modal matching, which aims to establish the correspondence between two different modalities, is fundamental to a variety of tasks such as cross-modal retrieval and vision-and-language understanding. Although a huge number of crossmodal matching methods have been proposed and achieved remarkable progress in recent years, almost all of these methods implicitly assume that the multimodal training data are correctly aligned. In practice, however, such an assumption is extremely expensive even impossible to satisfy. Based on this observation, we reveal and study a latent and challenging direction in cross-modal matching, named noisy correspondence, which could be regarded as a new paradigm of noisy labels. Different from the traditional noisy labels which mainly refer to the errors in category labels, our noisy correspondence refers to the mismatch paired samples. To solve this new problem, we propose a novel method for learning with noisy correspondence, named Noisy Correspondence Rectifier (NCR). In brief, NCR divides the data into clean and noisy partitions based on the memorization effect of neural networks and then rectifies the correspondence via an adaptive prediction model in a co-teaching manner. To verify the effectiveness of our method, we conduct experiments by using the image-text matching as a showcase. Extensive experiments on Flickr30K, MS-COCO, and Conceptual Captions verify the effectiveness of our method. The code could be accessed from `www.pengxi. me` .

## **1 Introduction**

As one of the most fundamental techniques in multimodal learning, cross-modal matching aims to bridge different modalities. In recent years, some cross-modal matching methods [19, 11, 7, 26] have been proposed based on Deep Neural Networks (DNNs), which achieved remarkable progress in a

> _†_ Corresponding author.

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

variety of applications, such as clustering [29, 24], image/video captioning [1, 44, 22], cross-modal retrieval [40, 19, 13], and visual question answering [9].

In general, most existing cross-modal matching methods embed different modalities into a common space wherein the similarity of positive cross-modal pairs is maximized and that of the negative ones is minimized. Although these methods have achieved promising results, their success depends on an implicit data assumption, _i.e._ , the training data are correctly aligned across modalities. For example, in the vision-and-language tasks, the text needs to accurately describe the image content, and vice versa. In practice, however, it is extremely expensive and time-consuming to annotate or collect such data pairs. Especially, considering the data collected from the Internet [35, 14], it is inevitable to collect some mismatched pairs which are wrongly treated as the matched ones. To the best of our knowledge, such a special noisy label (correspondence) problem has been ignored so far, which will remarkably degrade the performance of matching methods as shown in our experiments.

Figure 1: Noisy labels vs. Noisy Correspondence. We denote the noisy samples with red lines and clean samples with green lines. The traditional noisy labels mainly refer to the errors in category labels, while the noisy correspondence refers to the alignment errors in paired data. For the noisy correspondence in cross-modal matching, the true positive pair correctly guides the cross-modal matching, while the false positive pair causes incorrect supervision for training.

Based on the above observation, we reveal a new paradigm for the noisy labels, named noisy correspondence. Different from the traditional noisy labels, the noisy correspondence refers to the alignment errors in paired data rather than the errors in category annotations (see Fig. 1). To the best of our knowledge, there is no effort has been devoted to study this new problem and the closest paradigm might be the partially view-aligned problem (PVP) [12, 41]. However, PVP is remarkably different from noisy correspondence, and the latter is more practical than the former. To be specific, PVP focuses on that the cross-modal alignment is totally unavailable, whereas the noisy correspondence focuses on that some correspondences are incorrect. In addition, PVP assumes that some correctly aligned data are available for training, whereas our noisy correspondence assumes that the clean and noisy data are mixed.

To solve the noisy correspondence problem in cross-modal matching, we propose a novel method, named Noisy Correspondence Rectifier (NCR). Our method is based on the memorization effect of DNNs observed in [3, 39], _i.e._ , DNNs tend to learn the simple patterns before fitting noisy samples. Motivated by this empirical observation, NCR divides the data into two relative accurate data partitions, _i.e._ , “noisy” and “clean” subsets, based on their loss difference. After that, NCR employs an adaptive prediction function for label rectifying so that the false positives and the true positives could be identified from the “clean” and the “noisy” subsets, respectively. Furthermore, we propose a novel triplet loss for robust cross-modal matching by recasting the rectified labels as the soft margin.

The main contributions and novelties of this paper could be summarized as below. i) We reveal a new problem in cross-modal analysis, which is also a new paradigm for noisy labels, termed noisy correspondence. Different from the traditional noisy labels, the noisy correspondence refers to the alignment errors in paired data instead of the errors in category annotations. To the best of our knowledge, this work could be the first study on this problem. ii) To solve the noisy correspondence problem, we propose a novel method for learning with noisy correspondence, named Noisy Correspondence Rectifier (NCR). One major novelty of NCR is that the rectified label is elegantly recasted as the soft margin of a triplet loss so that the robust cross-modal matching could be achieved. iii) To verify the effectiveness of our method, we conduct experiments on the image-text matching task. Extensive experiments on three challenging datasets verify the effectiveness of our method in the synthesized and real noises.

2

## **2 Related works**

In this section, we briefly introduce some recent developments in cross-modal matching and learning with noisy labels.

## **2.1 Cross-modal Matching**

Most existing cross-modal matching works seek to learn a common space wherein different modalities are comparable. In general, existing works could be roughly divided into two categories: 1) Coarsegrained Matching. It often utilizes multiple neural networks to compute a global feature and each network is used for a specific modality [17, 37, 8]. For example, Kiros et al. [17] use a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) to obtain the image and text features, while enforcing the similarity of positive pairs larger than that of the negative ones. To further boost the matching performance, VSE++ [8] uses some representative negatives to improve the discrimination of the model. 2) Fine-grained Matching. It seeks to measure the fine-grained similarity for cross-modal matching [19, 21, 7]. For example, SCAN [19] proposes learning the latent semantic correspondence between the image regions and words that are extracted by bottom-up attention [1] and GRU, respectively. VSRN [21] adopts a graph convolutional network for semantic reasoning. SGRAF [7] proposes constructing a similarity graph to reason the similarity and adopting an attention filtration technique to eliminate the less-meaningful alignments. Recently Chun et al. [6] introduce a new paradigm for cross-modal matching, i.e. possible many-to-many correspondence that existed in the image and captions. To achieve this, they propose to use probabilistic representations to model the possible one-to-many correspondence.

Although promising results have been achieved in recent years, the existing methods heavily rely on the correctly aligned data. In practice, however, such well-matched data is expensive and timeconsuming to collect. Moreover, some recent works [35, 14] show that a large-scale dataset collected from the wild could remarkably improve the performance of the model. However, such a data will inevitably contain some mismatched pairs. Hence, it is highly expected to develop some methods which are robust against the noisy correspondence, which has not been studied as far as we know. Different from the many-to-many correspondence [6] between image and captions, NCR reveals the noisy correspondence problem which refers to the alignment errors of image-text pairs and proposes to eliminate the negative impact from noisy pairs for downstream tasks.

## **2.2 Learning with Noisy Labels**

To handle the possible noisy annotations in the training data, a large number of methods have been proposed and almost all of them focus on the classification task [36, 27]. To reduce the negative impact of the noisy labels, the existing works often resort to robust architecture design, regularization, loss adjustment, or sample selection methods. Here, we mainly introduce the last two approaches which are most related to this work. To be specific, the loss adjustment achieves robustness by adjusting the contribution of clean and noisy samples w.r.t. the loss. For example, Reed et al. [32] proposed a bootstrapping loss based on the model predictions for loss correction. Zhang et al. [45] provided some theoretical explanations for the label correction along with a new label correction algorithm. Different from the loss adjustment methods, sample selection methods aim to select clean samples from a noisy dataset. For example, Arpit et al. [3] showed that DNNs tend to learn simple patterns before fitting noisy samples, namely the memorization effect. Motivated from this, Arazo et al. [2] proposed treating the samples with small loss as the clean samples. To avoid the selection bias of clean samples, Co-teaching methods [10, 43] use the samples with small loss to iteratively train two networks. In recent, DivideMix [20] adopts the MixMatch method [4] for semi-supervised learning with the clean and noisy samples.

Unlike the above noisy label studies, this paper focuses on the noisy correspondence problem which considers mismatched multimodal data pairs instead of incorrectly annotated data points. Besides the difference in the problem, this work is also different from the aforementioned studies in the methodology. To be specific, in cross-modal matching, it is impossible to directly adopt these noise label learning methods to solve the noisy correspondence problem due to the following two reasons. First, most of the noisy label learning methods propose to use the model’s prediction for label rectifying in the scenario of classification, while it is intractable to directly predict the correspondence of given pairs in matching models. Second, even if we can rectify the noisy correspondence somehow,

## **3 The Proposed Method**

In this section, we elaborate on the proposed method, _i.e._ , Noisy Correspondence Rectifier (NCR) which could be the first work to solve the noisy correspondence problem in cross-modal matching. In Section 3.1, we introduce the co-divide module which splits the training data into the clean and noisy subsets. After that, we introduce how to rectify the labels with an adaptive prediction function in Section 3.2. Finally, we detail how to combine the co-divide and co-rectify modules to achieve robust cross-modal matching in Section 3.3.

## **3.1 Co-divide**

Without loss of generality, we first introduce the cross-modal matching task by taking the image-text matching as a showcase. Given the training data _D_ = _{_ ( _Ii, Ti, yi_ ) _}[N] i_ =1[, where] _[ N]_[is the data size,] ( _Ii, Ti_ ) is an image-text pair and _yi ∈{_ 1 _,_ 0 _}_ indicates that the pair belong to the same instance (positive) or not (negative). For the noisy correspondence case, it defines that an unknown portion of _D_ is mismatched, _i.e._ , ( _Ii, Ti_ ) is a negative pair but wrongly labeled as _yi_ = 1. To solve such a noisy correspondence problem, we propose NCR to achieve robust cross-modal matching.

To begin, we project the visual and textual modalities into a shared space via two modal-specific networks _f_ and _g_ , respectively. Then the similarity of the given image-text pairs is computed through _S_ ( _f_ ( _I_ ) _, g_ ( _T_ )). For simplicity, we denote _S_ ( _f_ ( _I_ ) _, g_ ( _T_ )) as _S_ ( _I, T_ ) in the following. Some early empirical studies [3] show that DNNs tend to first learn simple samples and then gradually fit the noisy samples. This so-called memorization effect of DNNs will lead to a relatively low loss for the clean samples. Motivated by this, we utilize the difference of loss distribution between the clean samples and noisy samples to divide the training data like [10, 43, 2, 20]. As observed in [10], it probably introduces error accumulation if the neural network is trained in a self-divide manner. To avoid such a situation, we adopt the co-teaching paradigm. Specifically, we individually train two networks _A_ = _{f[A] , g[A] , S[A] }_ and _B_ = _{f[B] , g[B] , S[B] }_ with different initializations and batch sequences. At each epoch, the network _A_ or _B_ will model its per-sample loss distribution with a GMM and divide the dataset into clean and noisy subsets which are then used for training the other network, _i.e._ , co-divide. Note that, before co-divide, a warmup process is conducted on all training data to achieve initial convergence with _Lw_ as defined in Eq. 2.

However, such a method requires to specify the threshold value, which is a daunting task because the optimal value is actually the similarity boundary of positive and negative pairs and hard to be manually specified. ## **3.3 Robust Cross-modal Matching**

Exiting cross-modal matching methods can only handle the binary labels which are incompatible with the soft labels rectified by NCR. To achieve robust image-text matching, we propose a novel triplet loss _Lsoft_ by recasting the rectified labels as the soft margin. Mathematically,

where _m_ is the curve parameter, and _y_ ˆ _i_ is the rectified label. The above formulation is designed to achieve the following goal, _i.e._ , _α_ ˆ _i_ will be assigned a small value if _y_ ˆ _i_ is close to 0, and a large value otherwise. Thanks to Eq. 6–7, the similarity of the pair ( _I, T_ ) will be larger than that of the negatives by an adaptive margin _α_ ˆ _i_ .

Despite the adaptive margin, another major difference between _Lw_ and _Lsoft_ is that _Lsoft_ will use the hard negatives which are the most similar negative pairs. Although the hard negatives are helpful in improving the performance, _Lw_ cannot be beneficial from it due to the existence of noisy correspondence. Specifically, it is expected that only the similarity of the true positives is larger than that of the hard negatives. However, in the case of noisy correspondence, the similarity of the false positives will also be larger than that of the hard negatives, thus leading to the unavailability of the hard negatives for _Lw_ during the co-divide stage. The detail of NCR is presented in Algorithm. 1.

## **3.4 Discussions on Matching Loss**

To achieve robust cross-modal matching with the refined soft labels, we design a soft Triplet loss by recasting the labels into soft margins. Recently, there are some works have been proposed to handle the soft labels in the matching model. For example, Wray et al. [38] recast the soft similarity into binary labels by directly setting a threshold on the predicted similarity. Kim et al. [15] proposes a log-ratio matching loss with a regularization defined by the label distance ratio, which is computed by the continuous labels. Liu et al. [25] introduces the hubness problem in image-text matching and proposes to consider all samples in a mini-batch and weights them according to both local and global statistics. Wray et al. [38] recasts the soft similarity into binary labels by directly setting a threshold on the predicted similarity. Different from them, NCR proposes to recast the rectified soft labels into

6

soft margins in the triplet loss by assigning large margins to the true positive pairs and small ones to the false positive pairs. As a result and more importantly, our loss is specifically designed to solve the noisy correspondence problem whereas existing ones are not.

## **4 Experiment**

In this section, we carry out experiments to verify the effectiveness of NCR in robust image-text matching. In the experiments, we use three benchmark datasets including Flickr30K [42], MS-COCO [23], and Conceptual Captions [35]. Among them, Conceptual Captions is with real noisy correspondence from the wild, and Flickr30K and MS-COCO are with simulated noisy correspondence.

## **4.1 Datasets and Performance Measurements**

Three datasets are used in our experiments. To be specific, Flickr30K contains 31,000 images collected from the Flickr website with five captions each. Following [19], we use 1,000 images for validation, 1,000 images for testing, and the rest for training. MS-COCO contains 123,287 images with five captions each. We follow the data partition in [19] which consists of 113,287 training images, 5,000 validation images, and 5,000 test images. As Flickr30K and MS-COCO are well annotated, we simulate the noisy correspondence by randomly shuffling the captions of training images for a specific percentage, denoted by noise ratio. Conceptual Captions is a large-scale data consisting of 3.3M images with a single caption each. As this data set is harvested from the Internet, about 3% _∼_ 20% correspondences are incorrect [35]. In our experiments, we use a subset of Conceptual Captions for evaluation, named CC152K. Specifically, we randomly select 150,000 samples from the training split for training, 1,000 samples from the validation split for validation, and 1,000 samples from the validation split for testing.

Following [19], for all images, we take the Faster-RCNN [33] detector provided by [1] to extract the top 36 region proposals of which each is encoded as a 2048-dimensional feature. For evaluation, we take the recall at K (R@K) as the measurement. In short, R@K is the fraction of queries for which the correct item is retrieved in the closest K points to the query. In the experiments, we report R@1, R@5, and R@10 for a comprehensive evaluation.

## **4.2 Implementation Details**

NCR is a general framework which could enable almost all existing cross-modal matching methods robust against noisy correspondence. To verify the effectiveness of our framework, SGR [7] is chosen to guarantee the robustness because it is the state of the art in image-text matching. In brief, the image regions and words are projected into a shared embedding space through a full-connected layer ( _i.e._ , _f_ ) and a Bi-GRU [34] ( _i.e._ , _g_ ), respectively. For the similarity function _S_ , it will compute the similarity between the given image and text by combining the local and global features with the help of a graph reasoning technique proposed in [18]. Due to the space limitation, we leave more details and results in the supplemental material.

We train our network using the Adam optimizer [16] with the default parameters and a batch size of 128. For fair comparisons, the networks _f_ and _g_ are the same with SGR, _i.e._ , the word embedding size is 300 and the joint embedding space size is 2048. In addition, we fix the margin _α_ = 0 _._ 2 and _m_ = 10 for the soft margin through the experiments. At the inference stage, we average the similarities predicted by network _A_ and _B_ for the retrieval evaluation. To avoid overfitting, we choose the best checkpoint in terms of the sum of the recalls on the validation set.

## **4.3 Comparisons with State of The Arts**

In this section, we conduct comparisons on the three datasets. The baselines include SCAN [19], VSRN [21], IMRAM [5], SGRAF, SGR and SAF [7]. For Flickr30K and MS-COCO, we report the results with three different noise ratios, _i.e._ , 0%, 20%, and 50%. In addition, we also report the results of SGR on the clean Flickr30K and MS-COCO by discarding the noisy pairs, denoted by SGR-C. Clearly, SGR-C is a quite strong baseline since the used data does not contain noisy correspondence. We do not report the results of SGRAF and SAF on the clean datasets since our framework only extends SGR in this paper.

7

When the noise rate is 0%, we directly refer to the results reported in the corresponding papers. For the noisy cases, we train the baseline models with the recommended settings three times and report the best result. Note that for SGR, we found it is very sensitive to the noisy correspondence, as shown in Table. 2. To obtain a desirable result, we experimentally employ a pre-training process to SGR (denoted by SGR*), namely, training the model with the vanilla triplet loss without hard negatives and then following the standard pipeline of SGR.

**Results on Flickr30K & MS-COCO** . Table 1 shows the quantitative results on Flickr30K and MS-COCO. Note that for MS-COCO, we only report the results by averaging over 5 folds of 1K test images due to space limitation, and leave the results on the full 5K test images in the supplemental material. From the results, one could observe that NCR is competitive to SGRAF in the noisefree case, namely, NCR could achieve state-of-the-art performance even though it is proposed to achieve robustness. When the data is contaminated by the noisy correspondence, NCR remarkably outperforms all the baselines by a large margin. Even comparing with SGR-C which is trained on the clean data, NCR improves R@1 by 2.2%, 3.1%, 2.3%, and 2.9% in these four valuations.

**Results on CC152K** . Table 2 shows the quantitative results on the CC152K which is with real noisy correspondences. From the results, one could see that our NCR consistently outperforms the evaluated models by a considerable margin in terms of all metrics. Specifically, NCR is 4 _._ 5% and 5 _._ 4% higher than the best baseline in terms of R@1 in text and image retrieval, respectively. Moreover, the large performance gap between SGR and SGR* shows the noise sensitivity of the original SGR.

## **4.4 Comparison to pre-trained model**

In this section, we perform comparison to the large pre-trained model CLIP [31]. In brief, CLIP is trained on a massive dataset harvested from the Internet and thus presumably has a lot of noisy

8

image-text pairs. Such a comparison is helpful in understanding, big data based model (CLIP) or noisy correspondence modeling technique (NCR), which one is more favorable to handle the mismatching problem. More specifically, CLIP claims that using hundreds of million data could ignore the existence of possible noise, while we believe that a well-designed algorithm is essential to solve the noisy correspondence. Noticed, although some existing works including CLIP have slightly/indirectly realized the existence of the noise, NONE of them explicitly give a solution to solve this problem and explores the characteristics of the noise correspondence.

In the experiments, we conduct CLIP on the MS-COCO dataset under the following two settings, _i.e._ , Zero-shot and Fine-tune. In brief, the first setting directly uses the released pre-trained CLIP to perform inference on MS-COCO, and the second fine-tunes the pre-trained model using the noisy training data of MS-COCO. As CLIP only released some pre-trained models and inference code[3] , we use the non-official code[4] to fine-tune the model with 32 epochs for the fine-tune setting. Note that CLIP (ViT-L/14 _[†]_ ) is unreleased and we report the results from the original paper [31]. One could observe that, although CLIP utilizes 400 million image-text pairs for pre-training, its performance inevitably degenerates during fine-tuning. In contrast, NCR achieves the matching performance with the presence of noisy correspondence, indicating the necessity of algorithm design.

## **4.5 Experimental Analysis**

In this section, we first conduct experiments to show the robustness and generalizability of the proposed method. Then, we investigate the effect of co-divide and co-rectify with the visualization results. After that, we carry out the ablation study to verify different components of NCR. Finally, we visually demonstrate some noisy cases detected by NCR.

## **4.5.1 Study on Robustness and Generalizability**

To show the robustness of NCR, we conduct experiments on Flickr30K by increasing the noise ratio from 0% to 60% with an interval of 10%. In addition, to verify the generalizability of NCR to other image-text matching methods, we extend SCAN [19] by NCR, denoted by NCR-SCAN. Fig. 4 shows that NCR and NCR-SCAN perform more stable than SGR and SCAN with increasing noise ratio. Moreover, NCR (NCR-SCAN) is remarkably superior to SGR (SCAN) in all tests, which shows the generalizability of NCR.

## **4.5.2 Visualization on the Co-divide and Co-rectify**

To further investigate the influence of the co-divide and co-rectify modules in our method, we carry out experiments on the Flickr30K dataset by visualizing the per-sample loss distribution and the model predictions on the noisy data, where the noisy ratio is 20%. For better visualization, here we show the result of NCR-SCAN and leave the result of NCR in the supplemental material. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the loss of most noisy samples is larger than the clean loss, which verifies the memorization effect of DNNs. By fitting the per-sample loss with GMM, NCR could effectively divide the data into clean and noisy splits. Regarding the analysis on the co-rectify, Fig. 3(c) shows

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper addresses a timely and significant challenge in multimodal learning—the problem of "noisy correspondence"—by arguing that existing cross-modal matching models are overly reliant on the assumption of perfectly aligned data. The motivation is conceptually strong, effectively distinguishing this paradigm from traditional noisy label research and the existing literature on partially view-aligned problems. The proposed method, Noisy Correspondence Rectifier (NCR), is logically sound, leveraging the well-established "memorization effect" of neural networks to partition data and effectively integrating these insights into a triplet loss framework. However, the clarity of the presentation could be improved by providing a more rigorous formalization early on; while the high-level intuition is clear, the document would benefit from a more precise mathematical definition of what constitutes a "noisy correspondence" versus a "mismatched pair" to ensure the scope of the method is well-defined. Overall, the work represents a high-quality contribution to robust multimodal learning, offering a practical, well-motivated solution to a prevalent real-world data challenge.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript tackles the practically significant and under-addressed problem of noisy correspondences in cross-modal matching, effectively distinguishing this alignment error from standard category label noise and proposing it as a novel noisy label paradigm. The motivation is compelling, particularly the focus on web-scale data where mismatched pairs are inevitable, and the distinction from partially view-aligned problems is clearly articulated; the writing is generally coherent, and the problem formulation is precise. However, the most significant concern regards the novelty and technical robustness of the proposed Noisy Correspondence Rectifier (NCR). While leveraging memorization effects and co-teaching is established in noisy label literature, the adaptation to cross-modal retrieval requires stronger justification regarding how the method mitigates confirmation bias in the presence of semantically plausible but incorrect pairs, which can mislead dual-network training and degrade retrieval performance. Furthermore, the claim of being the first study on this problem warrants caution, as related works in robust retrieval, loss-weighting for mismatches, and self-correction may overlap conceptually; a sharper theoretical and empirical comparison with these baselines is essential to solidify the contribution. Finally, the reliance on synthesized noise in preliminary evaluations may not capture the hardness of real-world alignment errors, necessitating rigorous validation on real-world noisy corpora to demonstrate practical utility.

comments.title: Contradictory Description of the Partially View-Aligned Problem (PVP)
comments.quote: To be specific, PVP focuses on that the cross-modal alignment is totally unavailable, whereas the noisy correspondence focuses on that some correspondences are incorrect. In addition, PVP assumes that some correctly aligned data are available for training, whereas our noisy correspondence assumes that the clean and noisy data are mixed.
comments.explanation: The authors attempt to distinguish PVP from their proposed noisy correspondence setup but present a logically tangled comparison. First, stating that PVP 'focuses on that the cross-modal alignment is totally unavailable' directly contradicts the subsequent sentence claiming 'PVP assumes that some correctly aligned data are available for training.' If alignment were truly totally unavailable, no correctly aligned data would exist for training. The intended contrast likely is that PVP relies on a separate, fully aligned auxiliary dataset (or dictionary), whereas NCR operates on a single mixed pool of potentially noisy pairs. Without clarification, a careful reader might struggle to understand the actual methodological distinction, potentially misjudging the novelty or experimental setup.

Examples include 'mismatch paired samples' (should be mismatched), 'there is no effort has been devoted' (syntactically incorrect), 'recasted' (non-standard), and 'two relative accurate data partitions' (ambiguous/grammatically incorrect). These do not alter technical meaning but reflect careless proofreading. They should be corrected for professional presentation.

comments.title: Questionable Claim Regarding Hard Negative Similarity Under Noisy Correspondence
comments.quote: Specifically, it is expected that only the similarity of the true positives is larger than that of the hard negatives. However, in the case of noisy correspondence, the similarity of the false positives will also be larger than that of the hard negatives, thus leading to the unavailability of the hard negatives for Lw during the co-divide stage.
comments.explanation: This claim serves as the core motivation for abandoning standard triplet loss (Lw) in favor of L_soft, but it lacks intuitive or empirical grounding. False positives are randomly paired cross-modal samples; their similarity scores typically follow a distribution centered near the dataset mean, rather than systematically exceeding the similarity of the *most similar* labeled negatives (hard negatives). Hard negatives are specifically mined as negatives closest to the anchor, making them relatively high-similarity samples. Asserting that random false-positive pairs will consistently surpass them is not generally true and depends heavily on dataset characteristics and embedding calibration. Without justification, this weakens the methodological motivation.

It states that the equation 'will use the original labels... to rectify the correspondence,' which implies modification, but no specific combination rule (e.g., weighting between yi and P(I,T)) is provided. For the noisy subset (Sn), it explicitly states to discard original labels and average predictions. The asymmetry in description, combined with the missing equation details, leaves the exact rectification procedure for Sc underspecified.

comments.title: Ambiguous Terminology and Typographical Errors
comments.quote: Even comparing with SGR-C which is trained on the clean data, NCR improves R@1 by 2.2%, 3.1%, 2.3%, and 2.9% in these four valuations. ... Exiting cross-modal matching methods can only handle the binary labels which are incompatible with the soft labels rectified by NCR.
comments.explanation: The results section uses the non-standard term 'valuations' to refer to experimental settings or noise ratios, which could confuse readers. Additionally, Section 3.3 contains a noticeable typographical error ('Exiting' instead of 'Existing'). While minor, these language issues should be corrected for clarity and professional presentation.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript tackles the practically significant and under-addressed problem of noisy correspondences in cross-modal matching, effectively distinguishing this alignment error from standard category label noise and proposing it as a novel noisy label paradigm. The motivation is compelling, particularly the focus on web-scale data where mismatched pairs are inevitable, and the distinction from partially view-aligned problems is clearly articulated; the writing is generally coherent, and the problem formulation is precise. However, the most significant concern regards the novelty and technical robustness of the proposed Noisy Correspondence Rectifier (NCR). While leveraging memorization effects and co-teaching is established in noisy label literature, the adaptation to cross-modal retrieval requires stronger justification regarding how the method mitigates confirmation bias in the presence of semantically plausible but incorrect pairs, which can mislead dual-network training and degrade retrieval performance. Furthermore, the claim of being the first study on this problem warrants caution, as related works in robust retrieval, loss-weighting for mismatches, and self-correction may overlap conceptually; a sharper theoretical and empirical comparison with these baselines is essential to solidify the contribution. Finally, the reliance on synthesized noise in preliminary evaluations may not capture the hardness of real-world alignment errors, necessitating rigorous validation on real-world noisy corpora to demonstrate practical utility.

comments.title: Contradictory Description of the Partially View-Aligned Problem (PVP)
comments.quote: To be specific, PVP focuses on that the cross-modal alignment is totally unavailable, whereas the noisy correspondence focuses on that some correspondences are incorrect. In addition, PVP assumes that some correctly aligned data are available for training, whereas our noisy correspondence assumes that the clean and noisy data are mixed.
comments.explanation: The authors attempt to distinguish PVP from their proposed noisy correspondence setup but present a logically tangled comparison. First, stating that PVP 'focuses on that the cross-modal alignment is totally unavailable' directly contradicts the subsequent sentence claiming 'PVP assumes that some correctly aligned data are available for training.' If alignment were truly totally unavailable, no correctly aligned data would exist for training. The intended contrast likely is that PVP relies on a separate, fully aligned auxiliary dataset (or dictionary), whereas NCR operates on a single mixed pool of potentially noisy pairs. Without clarification, a careful reader might struggle to understand the actual methodological distinction, potentially misjudging the novelty or experimental setup. This is primarily a framing/ambiguity issue in the introduction.

comments.title: Grammatical Errors Affecting Precision and Flow
comments.quote: Different from the traditional noisy labels which mainly refer to the errors in category labels, our noisy correspondence refers to the mismatch paired samples. To the best of our knowledge, there is no effort has been devoted to study this new problem... One major novelty of NCR is that the rectified label is elegantly recasted as the soft margin
comments.explanation: The passage contains several grammatical errors that slightly hinder precise reading: 'mismatch paired samples' should be 'mismatched paired samples', 'there is no effort has been devoted' is syntactically incorrect (should be 'there has been no effort'), and 'recasted' is non-standard (should be 'recast'). These do not alter the technical meaning but reflect careless proofreading. Given the leniency policy for introductory sections, I classify this as minor, but they should be corrected for professional presentation.

comments.title: Awkward Phrasing in Data Partitioning Description
comments.quote: Motivated by this empirical observation, NCR divides the data into two relative accurate data partitions, _i.e._ , “noisy” and “clean” subsets, based on their loss difference.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'two relative accurate data partitions' is grammatically incorrect and conceptually ambiguous. It likely intends to say 'two data partitions of relatively high accuracy' or 'two relatively accurately partitioned subsets'. As written, 'relative accurate' is not standard English and could momentarily confuse a reader about what property is being relative or accurate. This is a minor phrasing issue that does not affect the core methodology but could benefit from correction for clarity.

comments.title: Ambiguous output range for adaptive prediction function
comments.quote: where b is the batch size, Θ(·) clamps the elements into the range of [0, α], s is the similarity margin between the given pair (I, T) to the mean of the negatives in a mini-batch, τ is the average similarity margin of the largest 10% pairs in terms of s. This implies that the data have at least 10% clean pairs which could be regarded as a similarity anchor for prediction. Intuitively, the pairs with the similarity margin larger than τ would be predicted as 1, otherwise [0, 1).
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by the conflicting descriptions of the prediction function's output range. The mathematical setup explicitly states that Θ(·) clamps elements into [0, α], but the intuitive explanation immediately following claims predictions will be 1 or fall in [0, 1). This inconsistency raises a practical concern: since these rectified labels are subsequently used as soft margins in L_soft (Eq. 7), the exact range directly impacts loss scaling and gradient magnitudes. A careful reader might incorrectly assume the margins are in [0, 1) when they should be in [0, α], or vice versa. The authors should clarify whether α acts as a final scaling factor or if the prediction output is fundamentally bounded by [0, 1) before clamping.

comments.title: Questionable claim regarding hard negative similarity under noisy correspondence
comments.quote: Specifically, it is expected that only the similarity of the true positives is larger than that of the hard negatives. However, in the case of noisy correspondence, the similarity of the false positives will also be larger than that of the hard negatives, thus leading to the unavailability of the hard negatives for Lw during the co-divide stage.
comments.explanation: I first read this as the core methodological motivation for abandoning the standard triplet loss (Lw) during co-divide in favor of the proposed Lsoft. However, upon closer inspection, the claim lacks intuitive or empirical grounding. False positives are randomly paired cross-modal samples; their similarity scores typically follow a distribution centered near the dataset's mean or zero, rather than systematically exceeding the similarity of the *most similar* labeled negatives (hard negatives). Hard negatives are specifically mined as the negatives closest to the anchor, making them relatively high-similarity samples. Asserting that random false-positive pairs will consistently surpass them in similarity is not generally true and depends heavily on dataset characteristics and embedding calibration. Without justification, this weakens the claimed 'unavailability' of hard negatives for Lw and makes the switch to Lsoft appear less rigorously motivated.

comments.title: Indexing inconsistency in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: 3 W[A] = {wi[A]}_{i=0}^N ← GMM(D, B)
comments.explanation: While reviewing the algorithm pseudocode, I noticed that Step 3 initializes the weight vector W^{[A]} with an index range of i=0 to N. This contradicts the formal dataset definition in Section 3.1, where D = {(I_i, T_i, y_i)}_{i=1}^N. Starting indices at 0 instead of 1 introduces an off-by-one discrepancy that could mislead readers or implementers regarding batch indexing, sample alignment, and GMM fitting boundaries. Additionally, Step 6 uses a universal quantifier (∀) in a set-builder context that is syntactically awkward for standard mathematical notation. While likely an oversight, correcting the index range to i=1 would align the algorithm with the problem definition and improve reproducibility.

comments.title: Dataset split description error for CC152K evaluation
comments.quote: Specifically, we randomly select 150,000 samples from the training split for training, 1,000 samples from the validation split for validation, and 1,000 samples from the validation split for testing.
comments.explanation: Reading this carefully, I initially assumed the authors meant to draw the test set from the test split, as is standard in ML evaluations. However, the text explicitly states 'validation split' for both validation and testing. I checked the surrounding context and the standard practice for CC datasets (which typically have train/val/test partitions), but the passage itself contains a direct contradiction. If taken literally, the test evaluation would be measuring validation performance twice, or the authors inadvertently evaluated on a held-out validation portion instead of the designated test set. This is a procedural ambiguity that could mislead reproducibility or invalidate comparison claims. Given it's likely a typo, I assign moderate severity.

comments.title: Verbatim repetition of prior work description
comments.quote: Recently, there are some works have been proposed to handle the soft labels in the matching model. For example, Wray et al. [38] recast the soft similarity into binary labels by directly setting a threshold on the predicted similarity. Kim et al. [15] proposes a log-ratio matching loss with a regularization defined by the label distance ratio, which is computed by the continuous labels. Liu et al. [25] introduces the hubness problem in image-text matching and proposes to consider all samples in a mini-batch and weights them according to both local and global statistics. Wray et al. [38] recasts the soft similarity into binary labels by directly setting a threshold on the predicted similarity.
comments.explanation: The sentence describing Wray et al. [38] appears twice in immediate succession. My first thought was that this might be an intentional emphasis, but the surrounding context clearly lists distinct related works (Kim, Liu, then Wray again). This is almost certainly a copy-paste or OCR artifact that interrupts the logical flow of the related work summary. It does not alter the technical claims but creates unnecessary repetition. Minor severity.

comments.title: Inconsistent margin scaling (α = 0.2 vs m = 10)
comments.quote: In addition, we fix the margin _α_ = 0 _._ 2 and _m_ = 10 for the soft margin through the experiments.
comments.explanation: The authors set the hard margin α = 0.2 for Lw, but the soft margin parameter m = 10 for α̂_i. In cross-modal matching and triplet losses, similarities are typically normalized (e.g., cosine similarity in [-1, 1]) and margins are conventionally small (e.g., 0.1 to 0.5) to allow the loss to transition smoothly between active and inactive states. A margin of 10 is orders of magnitude larger than 0.2 and exceeds the typical dynamic range of similarity scores. This would likely cause the loss to be perpetually active (since S(neg) - S(pos) rarely exceeds 2), potentially destabilizing training or indicating a typo (e.g., m = 1.0 or 0.1). I checked the definition of α̂_i in the provided text, but the exact functional form is truncated by OCR. Regardless of the exact formula, the stark scale mismatch between the base margin and the soft-margin multiplier is suspicious and warrants clarification to ensure the loss behaves as intended. Moderate severity.

comments.title: Off-by-one indexing in Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 defines the collection of posterior probabilities as {w_i^A}_{i=0}^N. However, the dataset D is formally defined in Section 3.1 as {(I_i, T_i, y_i)}_{i=1}^N. In academic notation, indexing the collected posteriors starting from i=0 instead of i=1 creates a minor inconsistency, especially since no 0-th sample exists in the defined data. While this is easily resolvable in implementation (e.g., padding or adjusting indices), it could confuse a careful reader attempting to map the algorithm steps directly to the mathematical definitions. I flag it as minor since it does not affect the algorithm's logic or results. Minor severity.

comments.title: Inconsistent prediction function output range vs. theoretical clamping bound
comments.quote: Θ( _·_ ) clamps the elements into the range of [0 _, α_ ]
comments.explanation: Section 3.2 explicitly states that the adaptive prediction function $P(I,T)$ is clamped to the interval $[0, \alpha]$. The provided paper summary confirms that $\alpha$ is fixed at $0.2$. However, in Section 4.5.2, the authors report that 'the rectified soft labels of most clean pairs range into [0.3, 1] and those of most noisy pairs range into [0, 0.5].' This directly contradicts the $[0, 0.2]$ upper bound. If the clamping is strictly enforced as written, the reported ranges are mathematically impossible. Conversely, if the observation is correct, the clamping bound in Section 3.2 is likely a typo (probably intended to be $[0, 1]$, which is standard for soft label prediction). This mismatch creates confusion about the actual behavior of the co-rectify module and could mislead readers attempting to reproduce the label rectification step.

comments.title: Ambiguous terminology 'four valuations' in results description
comments.quote: Even comparing with SGR-C which is trained on the clean data, NCR improves R@1 by 2.2%, 3.1%, 2.3%, and 2.9% in these four valuations.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'four valuations' is non-standard in this context and initially raises a question about whether it refers to evaluation metrics, monetary valuations, or experimental conditions. Given the preceding sentence lists four percentage improvements, the authors almost certainly mean 'four experimental settings' (e.g., different noise ratios or dataset splits listed in Table 1). While the intent is clear from context, the word 'valuations' is a likely typo that could confuse a careful reader. It does not undermine the core results but introduces unnecessary ambiguity in the prose.

comments.title: Contradiction in prediction function output range
comments.quote: where _b_ is the batch size, Θ( _·_ ) clamps the elements into the range of [0 _,_ α_ ]... Intuitively, the pairs with the similarity margin larger than _τ_ would be predicted as 1, otherwise [0 _,_ 1).
comments.explanation: The text explicitly states that $\Theta(\cdot)$ clamps values to $[0, \alpha]$, and the provided summary confirms $\alpha=0.2$. However, the subsequent sentence claims the function predicts 1 when $s > \tau$. A clamping operation to $[0, 0.2]$ cannot produce a prediction of 1. This likely stems from conflating the similarity margin parameter $\alpha$ with the range of the prediction function. As a result, the exact scale of the rectified labels $\hat{y}_i$ is ambiguous. Since these soft labels directly determine the adaptive margin $\hat{\alpha}_i$ in the triplet loss, this inconsistency could mislead reproduction efforts regarding how true vs. false positives are weighted during training.

comments.title: Underspecified variable $b$ in prediction function description
comments.quote: where _b_ is the batch size, Θ( _·_ ) clamps the elements into the range of [0 _,_ α_ ], _s_ is the similarity margin...
comments.explanation: The variable $b$ (batch size) is introduced in the paragraph describing $P(I,T)$, but it does not appear in the surrounding text or the provided paper summary. It is almost certainly part of the missing equation for the similarity margin $s$ (e.g., computing the mean negative similarity over the batch). Without the explicit formula for $s$ or clarification on how $b$ is used, the precise computation of the adaptive prediction function cannot be reproduced.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 indexing typo
comments.explanation: The index for the collection of probabilities is written as $i=0$, but the dataset is defined as $D = \{(I_i, T_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^N$ in Section 3.1. Starting the index at 0 would either reference a non-existent sample or indicate an off-by-one error. Given the standard notation and the rest of the algorithm, this is almost certainly a typo for $i=1$.

comments.title: Vague mechanism for label rectification in clean subset
comments.quote: On the one hand, as most pairs of _Sc_ are true positive, Eq. 4 will use the original labels _yi[c]_ to rectify the correspondence.
comments.explanation: The description of how Eq. 4 rectifies labels for the clean subset ($S_c$) is logically incomplete. It states that the equation 'will use the original labels... to rectify the correspondence,' which implies modifying them, but no specific combination rule (e.g., weighting between $y_i$ and $P(I,T)$) is provided. For the noisy subset ($S_n$), it explicitly says to discard original labels and average predictions. The asymmetry in description, combined with the missing equation, leaves the exact rectification procedure for $S_c$ underspecified.

comments.title: Typographical error in Section 3.3
comments.quote: Exiting cross-modal matching methods can only handle the binary labels which are incompatible with the soft labels rectified by NCR.
comments.explanation: The word 'Exiting' is clearly a typo for 'Existing'. While this does not affect the technical content, it is a noticeable error in the manuscript that should be corrected for publication.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: Based on the beginning of the paper, the work addresses a timely and practically relevant problem—noisy correspondence in cross-modal matching—and introduces a novel paradigm distinct from traditional noisy labels. The clarity of the exposition is generally strong, with a well-structured abstract, clear motivation, and informative Figure 1 that effectively contrasts noisy labels with noisy correspondence. The proposed method (NCR) leverages the memorization effect to partition data and uses an adaptive prediction model in a co-teaching manner, which is a sensible adaptation from the noisy-label literature. However, a significant issue is that the paper does not yet provide a rigorous formalization of the noise model (e.g., what types of mismatches are considered, how noise rates are parameterized) nor a clear comparison with the closest related problem—partially view-aligned problem (PVP)—beyond a brief conceptual distinction. Additionally, while the authors claim to be the first to study this problem, the related work section is very brief and lacks discussion of potential alternative approaches (e.g., robust contrastive learning, outlier detection) that could be adapted. The experimental validation is only previewed (synthesized and real noises), but without any quantitative results or baselines in the current excerpt, the reader cannot assess the method’s efficacy. Finally, the writing style is occasionally informal (e.g., “remarkable progress,” “extremely expensive even impossible”) and could benefit from more precise language. Overall, the paper shows promise but requires stronger formalization, broader related-work coverage, and preliminary evidence to substantiate its claims.

comments.title: Underspecified parameter 'm' in soft margin calculation
comments.quote: where _m_ is the curve parameter, and _y_ˆ_i_ is the rectified label.
comments.explanation: In Eq. 7, the soft margin α̂_i is defined with a 'curve parameter' m, but no default value, range, or tuning strategy is provided in the passage. While the paper may discuss this in experiments, a reader reproducing the method would need to know how to set m or whether it is a hyperparameter (e.g., m=2 or m=0.5). This underspecification could affect reproducibility, but is common for hyperparameters that are later specified in experimental details. It is flagged as minor since it's likely addressed in Section 4.

comments.title: Missing equation content after 'where _Lw_ is defined as:'
comments.quote:
where _Lw_ is defined as:

where ( _I, T_ ) is a positive pair
comments.explanation: The passage introduces the per-sample loss computation and states 'where _Lw_ is defined as:' but the actual definition of Lw is missing; the text jumps directly to describing the terms. The same issue occurs for Eq. 4 and Eq. 6, where the equation bodies are absent. This appears to be an OCR extraction failure, not an author error. However, for a reviewer, the lack of these critical equations makes it impossible to verify the correctness of the loss formulation, the rectification procedure, and the soft margin calculation. Without the equations, a reader cannot assess whether the claimed contributions (adaptive prediction, soft margin triplet loss) are properly defined.

comments.title: Threshold set to 0.5 without justification for varying noise rates
comments.quote: By setting a threshold to {wi}[N] i=1[, we divide the data into clean and] noisy subsets. For simplicity, we set the threshold to 0.5 through all experiments.
comments.explanation: The authors state they set the threshold for clean/noisy division to 0.5 across all experiments 'for simplicity.' However, the optimal threshold likely depends on the noise rate of the dataset (e.g., 10% vs. 50% noise). In the noisy label learning literature (e.g., DivideMix), the threshold is often adaptively set or tuned. The authors claim this works 'through all experiments' but provide no empirical or theoretical justification that 0.5 is universally appropriate. This is an underspecified method choice that could affect reproducibility if the noise rate varies significantly.

comments.title: Unclear justification for 'at least 10% clean pairs' assumption
comments.quote: where b is the batch size, Θ(·) clamps the elements into the range of [0, α], s is the similarity margin between the given pair (I, T) to the mean of the negatives in a mini-batch, τ is the average similarity margin of the largest 10% pairs in terms of s. This implies that the data have at least 10% clean pairs which could be regarded as a similarity anchor for prediction.
comments.explanation: The adaptive prediction function P(I,T) relies on taking the top 10% of pairs by similarity margin s, and the authors claim this 'implies that the data have at least 10% clean pairs.' This assumption is questionable and not justified. In high-noise scenarios (e.g., 50% noise), the top 10% could still contain false positives. Moreover, the 10% value seems arbitrary. The authors do not discuss how this assumption might break down under varying noise rates or provide sensitivity analysis. This is a potential weakness in the method's robustness claim.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated comparison to SGR-C: SGR-C not defined in passage
comments.quote: Even comparing with SGR-C which is trained on the clean data, NCR improves R@1 by 2.2%, 3.1%, 2.3%, and 2.9% in these four valuations.
comments.explanation: The baseline 'SGR-C' is introduced here without prior definition in the provided context. From the paper summary, there is no mention of SGR-C; the only related baseline mentioned is 'SGR' (AAAI’21). The reader must infer that SGR-C likely refers to SGR trained on clean (noise-free) data, but this is not explicitly stated in the passage. This underspecification makes it difficult for a reader to reproduce the comparison or assess its fairness. The claim that NCR outperforms a model trained on clean data is notable, but the lack of clarity on what SGR-C is undermines confidence.

comments.title: Potential misalignment of metrics in comparison with CLIP
comments.quote: Table 3: Comparison with CLIP on MS-COCO 5K... 0%, Zero-Shot... CLIP (ViT-L/14_†_)... NCR... 58.2 84.2 91.5... 41.7 71.0 81.3
comments.explanation: The table header indicates '0%, Zero-Shot' for CLIP but NCR is not zero-shot; NCR is trained on MS-COCO training data (with some noise ratio?). The comparison is asymmetric: CLIP uses zero-shot evaluation on MS-COCO, while NCR is trained and evaluated on the same dataset. This is an apples-to-oranges comparison that could mislead a reader into thinking NCR outperforms a larger pre-trained model under the same conditions. In reality, NCR benefits from in-domain training. The text notes '0%, Zero-Shot' applies only to CLIP, but the table layout groups 'CLIP (ViT-B/32)' and 'NCR' under the same '0%, Zero-Shot' row without clarifying that NCR is not zero-shot. This ambiguity is significant because the paper claims to show the 'necessity of algorithm design' over large-scale pretraining, but the comparison is confounded.

comments.title: Inconsistent gain reporting for SGR vs SGR* in CC152K results
comments.quote: Moreover, the large performance gap between SGR and SGR* shows the noise sensitivity of the original SGR.
comments.explanation: The passage states there is a 'large performance gap between SGR and SGR*'. From Table 2, SGR achieves R@1 of 11.3 (image→text) and 13.1 (text→image), while SGR* achieves 35.0 and 34.9 respectively. The gap is indeed large. However, the meaning of 'SGR*' is not defined in the provided text; from the paper summary, SGR is a baseline, but SGR* is introduced only here. The reader must infer it is some variant (possibly trained on clean data or with a different protocol). Without definition, the claim about noise sensitivity is tenuous. Additionally, the paper summary mentions a noise-free case where NCR is 'competitive to SGRAF', but SGRAF is not listed in Table 2. There is inconsistency in which baselines are compared across tables.

comments.title: Underspecified training details for CLIP fine-tuning
comments.quote: we use the non-official code[4] to fine-tune the model with 32 epochs for the fine-tune setting.
comments.explanation: The passage relies on a non-official implementation for fine-tuning CLIP, but does not specify critical details such as learning rate, batch size, optimizer, or whether the same noise-corrupted training data was used as for NCR. Given that CLIP's performance drops dramatically (e.g., from 50.2 to 21.4 R@1 at 20% noise), it is plausible that the fine-tuning procedure was not optimized for robustness, potentially biasing the comparison in favor of NCR. The lack of methodological details makes it difficult for a reader to replicate or evaluate the fairness of this comparison.

comments.title: OCR misread: 'through:' at start of equation should likely be a verb
comments.quote:

The fragment 'through:

comments.title: Index bounds inconsistency in Algorithm 1 weights extraction
comments.quote: W[A] = {wi[A]}[N] i=0 ← GMM(D, B)
comments.explanation: The algorithm initializes the weight array index from i=0 to N, but typically dataset indices run from 1 to N. More critically, line 3 of the algorithm says 'W[A] = {wi[A]}[N] i=0' while line 4 uses 'W[B] = {wi[B]}[N] i=0', and later in lines 6-7 they iterate over (D, W^k) with N elements. Starting at i=0 instead of i=1 is inconsistent with the earlier notation in the paper where D = {(I_i, T_i, y_i)}_{i=1}^N. comments.title: Threshold set to 0.5 without justification for varying noise rates
comments.quote: By setting a threshold to {wi}[N] i=1[, we divide the data into clean and] noisy subsets. For simplicity, we set the threshold to 0.5 through all experiments.
comments.explanation: The authors state they set the threshold for clean/noisy division to 0.5 across all experiments 'for simplicity.' However, the optimal threshold likely depends on the noise rate of the dataset (e.g., 10% vs. 50% noise). In the noisy label learning literature (e.g., DivideMix), the threshold is often adaptively set or tuned. The authors claim this works 'through all experiments' but provide no empirical or theoretical justification that 0.5 is universally appropriate. This is an underspecified method choice that could affect reproducibility if the noise rate varies significantly.

comments.title: Unclear justification for 'at least 10% clean pairs' assumption
comments.quote: where b is the batch size, Θ(·) clamps the elements into the range of [0, α], s is the similarity margin between the given pair (I, T) to the mean of the negatives in a mini-batch, τ is the average similarity margin of the largest 10% pairs in terms of s. This implies that the data have at least 10% clean pairs which could be regarded as a similarity anchor for prediction.
comments.explanation: The adaptive prediction function P(I,T) relies on taking the top 10% of pairs by similarity margin s, and the authors claim this 'implies that the data have at least 10% clean pairs.' This assumption is questionable and not justified. In high-noise scenarios (e.g., 50% noise), the top 10% could still contain false positives. Moreover, the 10% value seems arbitrary. The authors do not discuss how this assumption might break down under varying noise rates or provide sensitivity analysis. This is a potential weakness in the method's robustness claim.

It is plausible that some of those losses could be adapted to noisy correspondence or implicitly provide some robustness. The claim may overstate the superiority of NCR's loss design. However, this is a common rhetorical device in papers to motivate novelty. Given that the authors have provided experimental comparisons showing NCR outperforms standard methods under noisy conditions, and the statement is in the discussion section (not a theorem), this is a minor overclaim rather than a major error. The context of the paper's contributions justifies that NCR is the first method explicitly designed for noisy correspondence.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'these four valuations' after reporting improvements

The phrase 'these four valuations' is vague — it should refer to four distinct evaluation settings (e.g., image-to-text and text-to-image on Flickr30K and MS-COCO). comments.title: Unsubstantiated comparison to SGR-C: SGR-C not defined in passage

From the paper summary, there is no mention of SGR-C; the only related baseline mentioned is 'SGR' (AAAI’21). The reader must infer that SGR-C likely refers to SGR trained on clean (noise-free) data, but this is not explicitly stated in the passage. This underspecification makes it difficult for a reader to reproduce the comparison or assess its fairness. comments.title: Potential misalignment of metrics in comparison with CLIP

In reality, NCR benefits from in-domain training. The text notes '0%, Zero-Shot' applies only to CLIP, but the table layout groups 'CLIP (ViT-B/32)' and 'NCR' under the same '0%, Zero-Shot' row without clarifying that NCR is not zero-shot. Given that CLIP's performance drops dramatically (e.g., from 50.2 to 21.4 R@1 at 20% noise), it is plausible that the fine-tuning procedure was not optimized for robustness, potentially biasing the comparison in favor of NCR. The lack of methodological details makes it difficult for a reader to replicate or evaluate the fairness of this comparison.

comments.title: Typo: 'noise label' instead of 'noisy labels'

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper addresses an underexplored problem in multimodal learning by formally introducing the concept of "noisy correspondence"—incorrect pairings in cross-modal training data rather than errors in class labels. The introduction effectively motivates this distinction from previous work on partial view alignment (PVP) and provides a clear, albeit brief, overview of the proposed Noisy Correspondence Rectifier (NCR), which relies on the memorization effect to separate clean and noisy data partitions. However, a key concern regarding novelty arises from the methodology: the use of loss-based data pruning is a standard heuristic in noisy label literature (e.g., Co-teaching), and the paper must strongly argue why the proposed approach—specifically the recasting of rectified labels as a soft margin in a triplet loss—offers a significant advantage over existing robust cross-modal matching methods. Additionally, while the problem definition is compelling, the manuscript needs to ensure that "noisy correspondence" does not overlap conceptually with other forms of noisy labeling, requiring a rigorous review of the literature to substantiate the claim that this is the first study on this problem.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 Implementation Error - Missing Labels in Sn
comments.quote: Sn[k] = { (Ii, Ti) | wi < 0.5 }
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the text explicitly states that for samples in the noisy subset, the original label $y_i$ is discarded and rectified using the model's predictions. However, in Algorithm 1, Step 7 defines $Sn[k]$ as only containing the image-text pairs $(I_i, T_i)$, excluding the ground truth labels $y_i$. This creates a logical gap: the algorithm attempts to 'rectify the labels' in Step 10, but the input data $B_j[n]$ (drawn from $Sn[k]$) lacks the target labels $y_i$ required for rectification based on the text's description.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 Description Contradicts 'Co-teaching' Principle
comments.quote: Sample a mini-batch (Bj[c], Bj[n]) from (Sc[k], Sn[k]); Train the network k on (B_^[c], B_^[n])
comments.explanation: The authors claim their method works in a 'co-teaching' manner where networks rely on the other network to identify clean samples to avoid selection bias (Section 3.1). However, the algorithm Step 9 explicitly selects mini-batches from $(Sc[k], Sn[k])$, which are the clean and noisy subsets *identified by network $k$ itself*. This implies network $k$ trains on its own clean partition, not the 'clean partition identified by the other network.' Furthermore, Step 10 states 'Train the network $k$ on...', which applies the training to the network that performed the selection, a standard self-supervised filtering approach rather than true co-teaching.

comments.title: Algorithm Notation Inconsistency (Sc vs Sn)
comments.quote: Sc[k] = { (Ii, Ti, yi, wi) ... } vs Sn[k] = { (Ii, Ti) ... }
comments.explanation: There is a direct inconsistency between the text in Section 3.2 and Algorithm 1 regarding what data is retained in the subsets. In Section 3.2 text, the definition of handling $Sn$ (false positives) implies the original label $y_i$ is present (as it is 'discarded' and rectified). In Algorithm 1, $Sc[k]$ is defined with $(I, T, y, w)$, but $Sn[k]$ is defined *only* with $(I, T)$. This difference makes it impossible to consistently implement the algorithm as described.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Label Rectification Logic
comments.quote: On the other hand, as most pairs of Sn are false positive, Eq. 4 will discard the original labels and rectify the labels by averaging the predictions P(Ii[n], Ti[n]) from the networks A and B.
comments.explanation: The text describes the mechanism of label rectification ($Eq. 4$) but does not provide the explicit mathematical formulation in the provided snippet (only a reference). Consequently, it is unclear how the method handles the case of a 'true positive' in the noisy subset $Sn$ (i.e., a correctly aligned pair). The rectification logic usually prevents 'true positives' from being moved to $Sn$ by the co-divide mechanism, but the interaction between the GMM thresholding and the final rectification step is not explicitly justified in the text provided.

comments.title: Missing Soft Margin Formula in Section 3.3
comments.quote: The soft margin $\hat{\alpha}_i$ is adaptively determined by: where $m$ is the curve parameter, and $\hat{y}_i$ is the rectified label.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce a novel triplet loss with adaptive margins ($\hat{\alpha}_i$) as a key contribution. However, immediately after stating that the margin is determined 'Mathematically' (or 'adaptively determined'), the text cuts off and merely lists the parameters ($m$ and $\hat{y}_i$) without providing the actual formula (presumably Equation 7 in the source). Without this equation, it is impossible to verify the derivation of the adaptive margin, check for mathematical correctness (e.g., divergence, scaling issues), or reproduce the method. This is a critical omission.

comments.title: Garbled Definition of Warmup Loss ($L_w$) in Section 3.1
comments.quote: where $L_w$ is defined as: where ($I$, $T$) is a positive pair, $\alpha > 0$ denotes a given margin, and $[x]+ = \max(x, 0)$. In the loss, the first term treats $I$ as queries taking over all negative text $T_{\hat{i}}$, while the second term treats $T$ as queries taking over all negative images $I_{\hat{i}}$.
comments.explanation: The definition of the warmup loss $L_w$ is presented in a fragmented and syntactically broken sentence structure. The text begins with 'where $L_w$ is defined as:', only to immediately re-introduce the text 'where ($I$, $T$)...' and then list the margin definition. This mangled formatting suggests that either a LaTeX equation block was incorrectly converted to plain text or an OCR error occurred, merging the definition with the margin explanation. This makes it difficult for the reader to distinguish the loss function components from the margin parameters, hindering verification of the mathematical formulation.

comments.title: Missing GMM Formula in Section 3.1
comments.quote: we fit the per-sample loss of all training data by using a two-component Gaussian Mixture Model [20, 30]: ... where $\beta_k$ and $\phi(\ell|k)$ are the mixture coefficient and the probability density of the $k$-th component, respectively.
comments.explanation: While the text describes the GMM parameters ($\beta_k$, $\phi(\ell|k)$) and the use of Expectation-Maximization, it explicitly omits the summation formula $P(\ell) = \sum_k \beta_k \phi(\ell|k)$ or the specific structure of the GMM. This prevents a mathematical review of the clean/noisy sample division mechanism. However, the textual description and the mention of standard GMM usage in Section 3.1 (co-divide) resolve the ambiguity sufficiently for the reader to understand the intent.

comments.title: Potential Notation/OCR Inconsistency in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: 3 $W[A] = \{w_i^{[A]}\}_{i=0}^N \leftarrow GMM(D, B)$
comments.explanation: In the provided text for Algorithm 1, Line 3 assigns $W[A]$ using a call to `GMM(D, B)`. However, the textual description in Section 3.1 explicitly states that the network should 'model ITS per-sample loss distribution' ('model ITS per-sample loss distribution with a GMM'). 'ITS' refers to the network performing the division (A or B). This suggests that A should model A's own loss distribution (implying `GMM(D, A)` or just `GMM(D)`). The argument `B` in `GMM(D, B)` contradicts the text's claim of self-dividing based on 'ITS' loss. While it could imply B's parameters define the distribution, the textual instruction regarding 'ITS' makes this notation potentially confusing or an OCR error.

comments.title: Missing Mathematical Formulations for Key Equations
comments.quote: where $L_w$ is defined as: ... The soft margin $\hat{\alpha}_i$ is adaptively determined by:
comments.explanation: The passage explicitly references and discusses specific mathematical formulas (Equation 2 for the warmup loss, Equation 4 for label rectification, and Equation 7 for the soft margin). However, the actual LaTeX or mathematical expressions for these equations are missing from the text block, replaced by descriptive prose. For example, for $L_w$, the text mentions a margin $\alpha > 0$ but does not write out the triplet loss equation. For the soft margin, it introduces parameters $m$ and $\hat{y}_i$ but omits the functional form (e.g., $\hat{\alpha}_i = (\hat{y}_i)^m + 1$). Without the explicit equations, it is impossible to verify the mathematical correctness of the derivations, check for missing terms (like the margin $\alpha$), or confirm the implementation of the soft margin logic (e.g., how a small $\hat{y}_i$ results in a small margin). This makes the paper unreproducible.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of 'Similiarity Margin' ($s$)
comments.quote: s is the similarity margin between the given pair (I, T) to the mean of the negatives in a mini-batch
comments.explanation: The variable $s$ is defined as 'the similarity margin'. In standard matching literature, $s$ usually represents the raw similarity score or the margin value itself (difference between positive and negative similarities). The phrasing 'similarity margin between (I, T) to the mean of the negatives' is slightly confusing. If $s$ is a margin, it should be a difference ($S(positive) - S(negative)$). The text implies this, but the term 'similarity margin' conflates the concept with the raw similarity. Furthermore, the subsequent sentence states 'τ is the average similarity margin of the largest 10% pairs in terms of s', which suggests $s$ is being treated as a value to be sorted. While contextually this likely defines a threshold for positive pairs, the terminology is imprecise.

comments.title: Vague Terminology: 'Per-Sample Loss'
comments.quote: fit the per-sample loss of all training data by using a two-component Gaussian Mixture Model
comments.explanation: The passage describes computing the 'per-sample loss' $\ell_i$. In the description of $L_w$, the term involves interactions with 'all negative text $\hat{T}$' and 'all negative images $\hat{I}$'. If the loss for a sample includes a summation or an explicit operation over a large set of negatives, the resulting scalar is not strictly 'per-sample' in the sense of a representative error value for that single instance; it often reflects the aggregate error of that instance against the rest of the dataset or batch. For GMM fitting, this aggregation must be consistent, but the term 'per-sample' is technically imprecise if the value represents a sum or complex interaction. It is clearer to refer to it as the 'sample-wise loss value'.

comments.title: Algorithmic Procedure: Rectification Logic Ambiguity
comments.quote: Rectify the labels of (Bj[c], Bj[n]) into (Bj^[c], Bj^[n]) using Eq 4–5
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 specifies that step 10 involves rectifying labels for both the clean ($S_c$) and noisy ($S_n$) subsets using Equation 4. However, the prose description of Equation 4 (Section 3.2) states that for $S_c$ (mostly true positives), it 'use[s] the original labels $y_i^c$ together with the model's prediction... to rectify the correspondence'. It is unclear what this rectification operation is. Does it average $y$ and $P$? Does it replace $y$ with $P$? The description is vague, though context suggests a consensus mechanism. Without the explicit Equation 4 formula, it is impossible to verify if this logic actually corrects errors in the 'clean' set (which should be reliable) or potentially corrupts it.

comments.title: Numerical Discrepancy in Baseline Performance Comparison
comments.quote: Even comparing with SGR-C which is trained on the clean data, NCR improves R@1 by 2.2%, 3.1%, 2.3%, and 2.9% in these four valuations.
comments.explanation: The authors claim NCR outperforms a clean baseline (SGR-C) by specific percentages (2.2% - 3.1%). However, Table 2, which presents results on the same dataset (CC152K), shows that NCR (R@1 = 33.1 for image retrieval and 29.0 for text retrieval) is strictly *worse* than the SGR* baseline (R@1 = 35.0 and 34.9), which is the clean/pretrained variant referred to in the context summary and logically equated to 'SGR-C'. Even if 'SGR-C' refers to the original SGR baseline (R@1 = 30.5, 26.9), the absolute difference for R@1 is only 2.6%, which does not match the cited 2.2% or 3.1%. The quantitative claim contradicts the provided data.

comments.title: Undefined Nomenclature 'SGR-C'
comments.quote: Even comparing with SGR-C which is trained on the clean data, NCR improves R@1 by 2.2%, 3.1%, 2.3%, and 2.9% in these four valuations.
comments.explanation: The provided context summary and Table 2 define notations only as 'SGR' and 'SGR*'. The passage introduces 'SGR-C' without definition, though the descriptor 'trained on the clean data' clearly aligns with the 'SGR*' variant. This undefined symbol creates confusion when cross-referencing Table 2, which lists 'SGR' and 'SGR*' but omits 'SGR-C'. While likely a typo, the missing definition makes the comparison ambiguous.

comments.title: Minor Phrasing in Novelty Claim

comments.explanation: The phrase "new direction in noise label" is linguistically awkward and technically imprecise. It likely intends to convey "new direction in noisy label learning" or "learning with noise labels." While the context of the paper (which proposes a 'new paradigm' in Section 1) resolves the ambiguity for a knowledgeable reader, the phrasing is not technically exact. It is a minor word choice issue rather than a structural or mathematical error.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of 'Similiarity Margin' ($s$)
comments.quote: s is the similarity margin between the given pair (I, T) to the mean of the negatives in a mini-batch
comments.explanation: The variable $s$ is defined as 'the similarity margin'. In standard matching literature, $s$ usually represents the raw similarity score or the margin value itself (difference between positive and negative similarities). The phrasing 'similarity margin between (I, T) to the mean of the negatives' is slightly confusing. If $s$ is a margin, it should be a difference ($S(positive) - S(negative)$). The text implies this, but the term 'similarity margin' conflates the concept with the raw similarity. Furthermore, the subsequent sentence states 'τ is the average similarity margin of the largest 10% pairs in terms of s', which suggests $s$ is being treated as a value to be sorted. While contextually this likely defines a threshold for positive pairs, the terminology is imprecise.

comments.title: Vague Terminology: 'Per-Sample Loss'
comments.quote: fit the per-sample loss of all training data by using a two-component Gaussian Mixture Model
comments.explanation: The passage describes computing the 'per-sample loss' $\ell_i$. In the description of $L_w$, the term involves interactions with 'all negative text $\hat{T}$' and 'all negative images $\hat{I}$'. If the loss for a sample includes a summation or an explicit operation over a large set of negatives, the resulting scalar is not strictly 'per-sample' in the sense of a representative error value for that single instance; it often reflects the aggregate error of that instance against the rest of the dataset or batch. For GMM fitting, this aggregation must be consistent, but the term 'per-sample' is technically imprecise if the value represents a sum or complex interaction. It is clearer to refer to it as the 'sample-wise loss value'.

However, the prose description of Equation 4 (Section 3.2) states that for $S_c$ (mostly true positives), it 'use[s] the original labels $y_i^c$ together with the model's prediction... to rectify the correspondence'. It is unclear what this rectification operation is. Does it average $y$ and $P$? Does it replace $y$ with $P$? The description is vague, though context suggests a consensus mechanism. Without the explicit Equation 4 formula, it is impossible to verify if this logic actually corrects errors in the 'clean' set (which should be reliable) or potentially corrupts it.

comments.title: Numerical Discrepancy in Baseline Performance Comparison
comments.quote: Even comparing with SGR-C which is trained on the clean data, NCR improves R@1 by 2.2%, 3.1%, 2.3%, and 2.9% in these four valuations.
comments.explanation: The authors claim NCR outperforms a clean baseline (SGR-C) by specific percentages (2.2% - 3.1%). However, Table 2, which presents results on the same dataset (CC152K), shows that NCR (R@1 = 33.1 for image retrieval and 29.0 for text retrieval) is strictly *worse* than the SGR* baseline (R@1 = 35.0 and 34.9), which is the clean/pretrained variant referred to in the context summary and logically equated to 'SGR-C'. Even if 'SGR-C' refers to the original SGR baseline (R@1 = 30.5, 26.9), the absolute difference for R@1 is only 2.6%, which does not match the cited 2.2% or 3.1%. The quantitative claim contradicts the provided data.
